rationale: The citing paper uses Novemsky et al. (2007) as direct empirical support for a key theoretical premise: processing difficulty can reduce willingness to choose. This premise is then carried into the paper's own argument that perspective conflict in charitable appeals creates fluency-related difficulty and lowers helping responses. Although the citation is brief, it is not just a background list item; it helps justify the mechanism behind the hypotheses.
relation_description: Hung and Wyer draw on Novemsky et al.'s preference-fluency evidence to motivate their claim that disfluency can suppress action tendencies. They apply this fluency logic to charitable appeal processing, where conflicting donor-victim perspectives are proposed to reduce appeal effectiveness.
